title: 'Doctor Who's Fate Is More Complex Than When BBC Pulled the Plug in 1989

blocks.content: Doctor Who hasn't been canceled – it's way more ambiguous than that. As a huge fan of the show, I can certainly understand the reaction of assuming the worst in the wake of the news that the 2026 Christmas special was never anything more than a ploy to keep the franchise alive. It was never actually going ahead. That doesn't mean Doctor Who as a whole is done, though. All it does is muddy the waters of when the Time Lord will be back on our screens.

I wasn't alive the last time Doctor Who was pulled, so I can only imagine how grim it must have been to lack new adventures for 16 years – ignoring a certain failed reboot movie in the '90s. The thought of enduring a stretch like that isn't something I want to think about. Thankfully, I highly doubt that will be the case this time. Although there are conflicting reports about how long Doctor Who's production break will be, the consensus seems to be that the BBC will only need a handful of years to reset and go again after the disastrous Disney era. What's confusing about that is that there is significantly more to solve compared to when the BBC axed the show in 1989.

Doctor Who's classic era ended with a three-part serial called "Survival." It's pretty ironic in retrospect, given the timing of the show being killed by the BBC. While never intended to be Doctor Who's farewell effort, "Survival: Part 3" functions perfectly well as a series finale. There's no cliffhanger as the Seventh Doctor (Sylvester McCoy) and Ace (Sophie Aldred) walk into the distance, chatting about the adventures still to come in the TARDIS. Even the last lines of dialogue seem purpose-written for a series finale. The Doctor's speech doubles up as the perfect way to end "Survival," as well as a brilliant way to leave things open and ongoing.

"There are worlds out there where the sky is burning, where the sea's asleep, and the rivers dream. People made of smoke, and cities made of song. Somewhere there's danger, somewhere there's injustice, and somewhere else the tea's getting cold. Come on, Ace, we've got work to do!" – The Seventh Doctor's final line in "Survival: Part 3"

By sheer coincidence, Seven and Ace vanish into the distance on their walk back to the TARDIS, with the implication that their adventures will simply continue. If Doctor Who had never been revived, it would have been perfectly fine to accept that the Doctor continued to cycle through his regenerations off-screen as he saved the day with a variety of companions. It was only when McCoy returned to hand off the baton to Paul McGann in 1996 that it was confirmed the Doctor had kept his Seventh face for quite a long time. Even when the Eighth Doctor's debut came to an end as his divisive movie expired, there were no significant loose ends. To end the franchise here would also have been fine. The same cannot be said for Doctor Who's most recent ending.
'Doctor Who' Has a Horrible Problem to Solve When It Comes Back

All Doctor Who had to do in 1996 was open the door for McCoy to come back, then have him regenerate into Paul McGann. Similarly, the more successful (but equally canon) revival in 2005 simply introduced Christopher Eccleston as the Ninth Doctor. It worked well. Long-standing fans could just assume McGann's regeneration happened off-screen, and newcomers had a perfect jumping-on point. For all the criticism being leveled at Russell T Davies lately, some of which I think is fair, it can't be denied that his work on bringing the show back in 2005 was brilliant. Sadly, I can't help but feel he's badly tarnished his Doctor Who legacy with how he's left the show in the wake of his more recent exit.

After two comebacks that allowed the story to continue with shocking ease in both 1996 and 2005, Doctor Who's next return must address that nonsensical Billie Piper twist. It was a clear attempt to maintain interest after Ncuti Gatwa's unexpected departure as the Fifteenth Doctor. Comments from both Piper and Davies have revealed that there was no underlying narrative reason or larger plan for Piper's return, this time as a character who is generally assumed to be the Sixteenth Doctor, but has never been officially confirmed as such. Even Gatwa confirmed during his recent appearance as the SNL UK host that he didn't get it either.

As anger-inducing and misguided as this casting stunt was, I also don't think it should be swept under the rug. If anything, that would feel even cheaper than bringing Piper back in the first place. I want a canon explanation, no matter how token it may be, as to why Fifteen's regeneration seemed to bring back Rose – or at least her face. At the same time, I don't want the twist to dominate any significant portion of the story when Doctor Who returns. I know that sounds like it's asking a lot, but that's the mindset Davies has forced upon me with such a random, detestable writing decision.
The BBC also seems unwilling to resume Doctor Who without a new production partner to replace Disney, showcasing a major shift in the show's behind-the-scenes process.

All of this is to say that I don't envy whoever follows Russell T Davies in the showrunner's chair. If Gatwa's final episode hadn't been so heavily reshot and had just been allowed to exist as an open-ended effort with Fifteen still in the TARDIS at the end, then the show would have been much easier to bring back than it currently is. With such a huge plot twist turning the Doctor into someone played by Billie Piper (who might still actually be the Doctor), Davies' successor has an uphill battle ahead to turn the development into something that's both rewarding and makes sense. I'm not saying Gatwa would necessarily have come back if Piper hadn't appeared, but at least there wouldn't be yet another regeneration quirk to account for. The show could just move on to the next Doctor. Good luck, whoever takes over Doctor Who next – I think you're going to need it.
Found an error? Send it info@movieweb.com so it can be corrected.

Originally premiered in 1963, Doctor Who is a sci-fi series that follows a powerful being known as a Time Lord, referred to as the Doctor. Using an interdimensional time-traveling ship known as the TARDIS, the Doctor travels time and space with various companions as they solve multiple problems and help avert catastrophe as much as they almost cause it. Though the Doctor is always the same character, they experience regenerations, allowing them to be recast every few seasons as a unique immortal being with new personality traits.
